coredata.dc:title: A novel dual-excitation and dual-emission fluorescent probe (CQDs-O-NBD) based on carbon quantum dots for detection and discrimination of Cys/Hcy and GSH/H2S in living cells
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jie
Jia, Haishuang
Liu, Wenfang
Wang, Jun
Fang, Dawei
coredata.dc:description: Cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy), glutathione (GSH) and hydrogen sulfide (H2S) are essential active substance in human body. The identification and detection of biothiols and H2S are of great significance for the early diagnosis of certain diseases. In this paper, a new dual excitation and dual emission fluorescent probe, carbon quantum dot-O-7-nitrobenzo-2-oxa-1,3-diazole (CQDs-O-NBD), for the detection of biothiols and H2S was synthesized. The carbon quantum dots (CQDs-OH) (λex/em maxima = 377 nm/467 nm) were synthesized by a microwave-assisted method using hydroquinone as the carbon source. Through the reaction of the phenolic hydroxyl group of the carbon quantum dots and 4-chloro-7-nitrobenzo-2-oxa-1,3-diazole (NBD-Cl), the CQDs-O-NBD was synthesized. Due to the static quenching (SQ) and the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) from CQDs to NBD, the fluorescence of the CQDs-O-NBD is very weak. After adding biothiols and H2S, the ether bond of the CQDs-O-NBD was broken through nucleophilic substitution reaction, which led to the release of the CQDs-OH (channel 1 is turned on) and generation of a new compound NBD-thiols (channel 2). The CQDs-O-NBD as a probe shows the high selectivity and sensitivity for Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS (LOD of 0.24 μM/0.21 μM/0.19 μM/0.18 μM) over other aminos and ions by channel 1. The new formed compounds from NBD and Cys/Hcy have strong fluorescence under 470 nm wavelength light excitation (channel 2 is turned on), while the formed compounds from NBD and GSH/NaHS have no fluorescence (no channel 2), so the CQDs-O-NBD can discriminate Cys/Hcy (LOD: 0.07 μM/0.06 μM) from GSH/NaHS. In addition, the CQDs-O-NBD shows low toxicity and can be applied to the detection of Cys in fetal bovine serum and imaging in living cells.

XPS survey (a) of the CQDs-OH, high-resolution XPS survey scan of the C 1s (b) and O 1s (c), FT-IR spectra (d) of NBD-Cl, the CQDs-OH and the CQDs-O-NBD.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of the CQDs-OH and NBD, maximum excitation and emission spectra of the CQDs-OH, (b) fluorescence spectra at different excitation wavelengths from 317 nm to 397 nm (with an interval of 10 nm) and (c) 3D fluorescence spectrum of the CQDs-OH. (d) Fluorescence decay curves of CQDs-OH and CQDs-O-NBD (10 μg mL−1) in ethanol at λex = 377 nm.
XPS survey (a) of the CQDs-O-NBD and high-resolution XPS survey scan (b) of the N 1s.

UV–vis absorption spectra (a) of the CQDs-O-NBD (10 μg mL−1) before and after reaction with Cys (30 μM), GSH (30 μM), Hcy (30 μM) and NaHS (30 μM); Photographs (b) of the CQDs-O-NBD with Cys, GSH, Hcy, and NaHS under the natural light and UV light conditions (a hand-held ultraviolet lamp); Fluorescence spectra of the CQDs-O-NBD (10 μg mL−1) before and after reaction with Cys (30 μM), GSH (30 μM), Hcy (30 μM) and NaHS (30 μM) for 120 min (λex = 377 nm) (c) and for 60 min (λex = 470 nm) (d) in ethanol/PBS aqueous solution (vethanol/vPBS = 1/1, CPBS = 10 mM and pH = 7.4) at the room temperature.

Fluorescent spectral changes and fluorescence intensity changes of the CQDs-O-NBD solution (10 μg mL−1) in the presence of (a) Cys (0–30 μM), (b) Hcy (0–27.5 μM), (c) GSH (0–27.5 μM) and (d) NaHS (0–27.5 μM). Insert: the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs-O-NBD (10 μg mL−1) as a function of the concentrations of Cys, Hcy, GSH and NaHS. Condition: in ethanol/PBS aqueous solution (vethanol/vPBS = 1/1, CPBS = 10 mM and pH = 7.4) for 120 min at the room temperature, λex = 377 nm. Error bars represent ± SD of three experiments.

Fluorescent spectral changes (column 1) and fluorescence intensity changes (column 2) of the CQDs-O-NBD solution (10 μg mL−1) upon addition of different concentrations of Cys (a) and Hcy (b). Insert: The linear relationships between fluorescence intensity and corresponding Cys/Hcy concentrations. Condition: in ethanol/PBS aqueous solution (vethanol/vPBS = 1/1, CPBS = 10 mM and pH = 7.4) for 60 min at the room temperature, λex = 470 nm. Error bars represent ± SD of three experiments.

The time-dependent fluorescence intensities of the CQDs-O-NBD (10 μg mL−1) with and without Cys (15 μM), Hcy (15 μM), GSH (15 μM) and NaHS (15 μM) in ethanol/PBS aqueous solution (vethanol/vPBS = 1/1, CPBS = 10 mM and pH = 7.4) (a: λex/em = 377/467 nm, b: λex/em = 470/546 nm).

The pH-dependent fluorescence intensities of the CQDs-O-NBD solution (10 μg mL−1) with and without Cys (30 μM), Hcy (30 μM), GSH (30 μM) and NaHS (30 μM) in ethanol/PBS aqueous solution (vethanol/vPBS = 1/1, CPBS = 10 mM and pH = 7.4), a: λex/em = 377/467 nm, b: λex/em = 470/546 nm. Error bars represent ± SD of three experiments.

Fluorescence responses of the CQDs-O-NBD solution (10 μg mL−1) to biothiols (30 μM) and some relevant analytes (100 μM) (1) none, (2) Cys, (3) Hcy, (4) GSH, (5) NaHS, (6) Arg, (7) Asp, (8) Glu, (9) His, (10) Lys, (11) Ser, (12) Thr, (13) Trp, (14) Tyr, (15) Val, (16) Gly, (17) Phe, (18) Ala, (19) Pro, (20) Iso, (21) Leu, (22) Br−, (23) CO3 2−, (24) HCO3 −, (25) ClO−, (26) Ca2+, (27) Fe3+, (28) Mg2+, (29) Pb2+, (30) Zn2+, (31) K+, (32) Na+, (33) NO3 −, (34) PO4 3−, (35) I−, (36) CH3COO−, (37) Vitamin C. All experiments were carried out in ethanol/PBS aqueous solution (vethanol/vPBS = 1/1, CPBS = 10 mM and pH = 7.4). Each spectrum was measured for 120 min (λex = 377 nm) and 60 min (λex = 470 nm) after the analytes were added. Error bars represent ± SD of three experiments.
The proposed reaction mechanism of CQDs-O-NBD with Cys/Hcy and GSH/NaHS.
Viability of HeLa cells based on MTT assay at different concentrations of CQDs-O-NBD (0–50 μg mL−1) for 12 h.

Confocal fluorescence imagings of HeLa cells. (Blue channel (λex = 405 nm and λem = 420–550 nm) (column 1), green channel (λex = 488 nm and λem = 520–570 nm) (column 2), bright field (column 3) and overlay field (column 4) in the presences of probe (a–d), NEM + probe (e–h), NEM + probe + Cys (i–l) and NEM + probe + GSH (m–p). Scale bar: 20 μm)).
Determination of Cys in fetal bovine serum (λex = 470 nm).

head.ce:title: A novel dual-excitation and dual-emission fluorescent probe (CQDs-O-NBD) based on carbon quantum dots for detection and discrimination of Cys/Hcy and GSH/H2S in living cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy), glutathione (GSH) and hydrogen sulfide (H2S) are essential active substance in human body. The identification and detection of biothiols and H2S are of great significance for the early diagnosis of certain diseases. In this paper, a new dual excitation and dual emission fluorescent probe, carbon quantum dot-O-7-nitrobenzo-2-oxa-1,3-diazole (CQDs-O-NBD), for the detection of biothiols and H2S was synthesized. The carbon quantum dots (CQDs-OH) (λex/em maxima = 377 nm/467 nm) were synthesized by a microwave-assisted method using hydroquinone as the carbon source. Through the reaction of the phenolic hydroxyl group of the carbon quantum dots and 4-chloro-7-nitrobenzo-2-oxa-1,3-diazole (NBD-Cl), the CQDs-O-NBD was synthesized. Due to the static quenching (SQ) and the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) from CQDs to NBD, the fluorescence of the CQDs-O-NBD is very weak. After adding biothiols and H2S, the ether bond of the CQDs-O-NBD was broken through nucleophilic substitution reaction, which led to the release of the CQDs-OH (channel 1 is turned on) and generation of a new compound NBD-thiols (channel 2). The CQDs-O-NBD as a probe shows the high selectivity and sensitivity for Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS (LOD of 0.24 μM/0.21 μM/0.19 μM/0.18 μM) over other aminos and ions by channel 1. The new formed compounds from NBD and Cys/Hcy have strong fluorescence under 470 nm wavelength light excitation (channel 2 is turned on), while the formed compounds from NBD and GSH/NaHS have no fluorescence (no channel 2), so the CQDs-O-NBD can discriminate Cys/Hcy (LOD: 0.07 μM/0.06 μM) from GSH/NaHS. In addition, the CQDs-O-NBD shows low toxicity and can be applied to the detection of Cys in fetal bovine serum and imaging in living cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy), glutathione (GSH) and hydrogen sulfide (H2S) are some sulfur-containing active substances and widely exist in organisms [1–3]. They play the important roles in human physiology processes, such as reversible redox reaction and metal ions chelation [2,4,5]. Cys, a precursor of GSH, is an essential amino acid in the synthesis process of protein [6]. Cys deficiency in human body may cause some diseases, such as growth retardation of children, liver injury, hair discoloration, skin damage and drowsiness [7,8]. While abnormal level of Hcy is closely related to Alzheimer's diseases, cardiovascular disease and neuropsychiatric disease [9]. GSH is the most abundant non-protein thiol in cells, and mainly exists in the form of reduction in the body. Imbalance of GSH concentration is bound up with cancer, heart disease, neurodegenerative diseases, diabetes and AIDS [10–12]. H2S is the most simple sulfhydryl species and a gasotransmitter, but it plays an important role in intracellular redox regulation and signal transduction [13–15]. Abnormal H2S concentration is associated with ovarian cancer, diabetes, breast cancer, hypertension and liver inflammation [13,16,17]. Therefore, the recognition and detection of these sulfhydryl compounds are of great significance for the early diagnosis of certain diseases.

The traditional methods for biothiols detection include capillary electrophoresis [18,19], electrochemical [20,21], high performance liquid chromatography [22,23], mass spectrometry [24], Raman spectroscopy [25]. These methods usually require expensive equipment, time-consuming pretreatment, and cell or tissue destruction. Compared with other methods, fluorescence probe has attracted great interest of researchers due to its simple operation, low cost, high resolution, high sensitivity and non-invasiveness [26–32]. Based on the chemical reaction of sulfhydryl groups with certain functional groups is one of the strategies for designing biothiols fluorescent probes [33–40].

Recently, the biothiols fluorescent probes based on 7-nitrobenzo-2-oxa-1,3-diazole (NBD) as a leaving group have received widespread attention [41–45]. Pluth group [46] reported the strategy for Cys/Hcy and GSH/H2S detection and discrimination based on small molecule probe. In brief, through the reaction of NBD-Cl with the phenolic hydroxyl groups of the fluorophore, the fluorophore is quenched mainly due to the PET effect. After adding Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS, the ether bonds of the fluorophore-O-NBD are broken, releasing the fluorophore (channel 1 is turned on). At the same time, the compound formed by NBD group and Cys/Hcy have strong fluorescence under 470 nm excitation (channel 2 is turned on), while the compound formed by NBD group and GSH/H2S has no fluorescence (no channel 2), which can be used to distinguish the GSH/H2S from Cys/Hcy. Most of the above-mentioned probes for detecting biothiols were constructed from organic fluorophores. In general, some probes have complex structures, time-consuming synthesis, poor photostability and long-term imaging difficulties [33]. Therefore, it is an urgent problem to find fluorophores with better optical properties, low toxicity and biocompatibility to construct new fluorescent probes for the detection of biothiols and H2S.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new type of fluorescent materials with sizes of less than 10 nm, have many advantages: low toxicity, biocompatibility, environmental friendliness, strong luminescent ability and optical stability. There are many methods to synthesize carbon quantum dots, including hydrothermal [47], solvothermal [48], reflux [49], ultrasound treatment [50] and microwave [51]. Due to the advantages of simple operation, short reaction time and low cost, microwave method has attracted more attention. The CQDs have good application prospects in biosensor, chemical sensing, biomedical imaging, cancer treatment, drug delivery, catalysis and LED [52]. Due to a lot of –NH2, –OH and –COOH groups, the surface of CQDs is more easy to be functionalized by various ligands, which endows CQDs better optical properties and more reaction sites for specific recognition of some substances. So far, various used ligands, such as organic molecules, ions, proteins, polymers and biological molecules, have been reported [52]. Wang et al. designed and synthesized a turn-on nonfluorescent probe for the selenol detection in living cells by linking carbon dots (CDs) with 2,4-Dinitrophenylsulfonyl (DNBS) through sulfonamide bonds [53]. An et al. constructed a single-excitation and dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor by coupling carbon dots (CDs) and 7-amino-4-methylcoumarin (AMC) via amide bonds, and applied it to the dopamine detection in human serum [54]. Liu et al. fabricated a dual-excitation ratiometric fluorescent probe based on a N,S-CDs-PEI hybrid for chlorogenic acid assay [55]. These references showed that the surface functionalization of carbon quantum dots is an effective way to obtain more selectivity to target analytes.

In this work, a novel dual-excitation and dual-emission fluorescence probe CQDs-O-NBD was designed and synthesized for detection and discrimination of Cys/Hcy and GSH/H2S. The fluorescence of the CQDs-OH is quenched by linking NBD groups forming nonfluorescent CQDs-O-NBD due to static quenching and the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) from CQDs to NBD. After adding biothiols and H2S to the CQDs-O-NBD solution, the ether bonds are broken via a nucleophilic aromatic substitution (SNAr). Under the excitation of 377 nm wavelength light the emitted blue fluorescence of the released CQDs-OH is used as channel 1 to identify biothiols from other substances. After an intramolecular rearrangement, the generated nonfluorescent NBD-S-Cys/Hcy was immediately transformed into the green fluorescent NBD-N-Cys/Hcy, while the nonfluorescent NBD-S-GSH/H2S did not undergo further transformation. So, under the excitation of 470 nm wavelength light the emitted green fluorescence of the generated NBD-N-Cys/Hcy is used as channel 2 to distinguish Cys/Hcy from GSH/H2S. Because the generated NBD-SH has a unique red color [56], H2S can be selectively identified by colorimetry. Compared with single-emission probes, the dual-emission probes can bring a high detection accuracy and an anti-interference ability. As a dual-excitation and dual-emission fluorescence probe, the CQDs-O-NBD has the good sensitivity and selectivity for biothiols and H2S in both two channels and can be applied for determining Cys content in fetal bovine serum and biological imaging in living cells.

Hydroquinone (p-C6H6O2), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), N,N-dimetylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), N,N-diisopropylethylamine (DIPEA), ethanol (C2H5OH) and 4-Chloro-7-nitrobenzo-2-oxa-1,3-diazole (NBD-Cl) were purchased from Alfa Aesar. All Other reagents were of analytical grade and purchased from Shanghai Aladdin biological technology (China) for directly use without further purification. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 20 mM) was made from Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4 and NaCl. The ultrapure water was used in the experiment (18.3 MΩ cm, Hitech-science tool, Shanghai, China). The HeLa cells were purchased from the Shanghai cell bank of Chinese academy of sciences.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on an IRAffinity-1 (Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Cary 50 (Varian Company, USA). All fluorescence spectra were performed on a Cary 300 fluorescence spectrometer (Varian Company, USA). Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM, Talos F200X, USA FEI). X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) were obtained on a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD (Kratos UK). X-ray diffraction patterns were obtained from X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD, D-8, Bruker-axs, Germany). Time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) data were obtained on a Harp 300 system (Picoquant). Mass spectrometry data were obtained on an ultra performance liquid chromatography (Waters, H-Class) coupled with a Time-of-flight (TOF) mass spectrometer (Waters G2-XS Qtof). A Global digital pH meter (FE20K, Mettler-Toledo Company, Switzerland) was used to measure the pH values of the solutions.

According to the previous report [51], CQDs-OH were synthesized by a microwave-assisted method with appropriate modification. 1.000 g p-C6H6O2 and 100 μL H2SO4 were dissolved in 2 mL ultrapure water. Then the mixed solution was heated in the domestic microwave oven. When the color of the solution turns dark black, stop heating and cool the product to room temperature naturally. The product was dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure, then transferred to a dialysis bag, and dialyzed for 12 h. The solution was freeze-dried to obtain dark green powder.

It was found that the reaction solvent and acid acceptor had a great influence on the reaction results. In the initial experiments, the researcher tried to use ethanol, dichloromethane, tetrahydrofuran, acetonitrile, DMSO or DMF as reaction solvents, and use trimethylamine, K2CO3 or DIPEA as acid acceptors. Finally, only the combination of DMF-DIPEA can successfully connect carbon quantum dots with NBD groups.

0.10 g CQDs-OH and 0.06 g NBD-Cl was dissolved in 2.0 mL anhydrous DMF, and then 130 μL DIPEA was added. Ethyl acetate was used to purify the probe CQDs-O-NBD because of the poor solubility of the probe and the good solubility of excess NBD-Cl, DMF, and DIPEA in ethyl acetate. After reacting at room temperature for 24 h, 20 mL of ethyl acetate was added and dispersed by ultrasound. Centrifuge the solution at 10000 rpm for 15 min, pour off the upper layer. Repeat the above steps until the ethyl acetate layer mixed with the NaHS solution does not show red, which indicates that all the NBD-Cl in the product have been washed away (NBD-Cl can react very sensitively with H2S and show red.). Afterwards, NBD-modified carbon quantum dots can be obtained by drying.

According to Ref. [57], The fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of CQDs-OH and CQDs-O-NBD were calculated by a relative method. The quinine sulfate (QY = 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4) was taken as the reference sample. According to the formula:
Y = Yst(mx/mst) (ηx 2/ηst 2)
Where Y is the quantum yield, m is the slope, η is the refractive index of the solvent, st for standard and x for sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of various solutions and measurements of spectra
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs-O-NBD was dissolved in DMF to prepare a stock solution (1.0 mg mL−1). Cys, Hcy, GSH and NaHS (a standard source for H2S) were dissolved in ultra-pure water to prepare stock solutions (1.0 mM). The solutions of interfering substances including amino acids (Arg, Asp, Glu, His, Lys, Ser, Thr, Trp, Tyr, Val, Gly, Phe, Ala, Pro, Iso and Leu) and various ions (Br−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, ClO−, Ca2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, K+, Na+, NO3 −, PO4 3−, I−, CH3COO− and Vitamin C) were dissolved in ultra-pure water. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) were prepared according to literature [58,59]. First, the test solution of the probe CQDs-O-NBD (10 μg mL−1) was prepared in ethanol/PBS aqueous solution (vethanol/vPBS = 1/1, CPBS = 10 mM and pH = 7.4), then 2.0 mL of this solution was added to the standard quartz cell. After adding biothiols or interfering substances, the samples were incubated for a certain time at room temperature, and then the UV–vis or fluorescence spectra were recorded.

A MTT assay was used to evaluate the toxicity of the CQDs-O-NBD to living cells. Hela cells were cultured in 96 well plates for 24 h using Dulbecco's modified eagle's medium (DMEM) medium (10% fetal bovine serum) (37 °C and 5.0% CO2). After removing the medium, each well was incubated with different concentrations of probe (0–50 μg mL−1) for 12 h. After that, 15 μL MTT (5.0 mg mL−1) was added to culture for 4.0 h. Finally, 100 μL DMSO was added into each well to dissolve the formed methoxyaza crystal. The plates were stirred for 10 min, and then the absorbance of each well was measured at 570 nm using a Thermo Scientific reader. Five experiments were performed for each individual concentration, and the mean ± standard deviation of the dates were used to evaluate cells viability.

The Hela cells were treated in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) at 37 °C in humid air containing 5.0% CO2 for 24 h. The DMEM was supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 100 U mL−1 penicillin, and 100 μg mL−1 streptomycin. Then the medium was removed and the cells were washed for three times with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) (pH 7.4). In the first group of experiments, the cells were incubated with the 20 μg mL−1 probe for 4.0 h. In the second group of experiment, the cells were cultured with N-ethylmaleimide (NEM, 1.0 mM) at 37 °C for 40 min, then incubated with the probe for 4.0 h. In the third and fourth groups of experiments, the cells pretreated with N-ethylmaleimide (NEM, 1.0 mM) at 37 °C for 40 min, then incubated with probes for 4.0 h, and then incubated with 100 μM Cys or GSH for 1.0 h. The cells of all groups were washed with PBS three times, then the cell imagings were obtained with a confocal laser scanning microscope (OLYMPUS FV1200) at excitation wavelength of 405 nm and 488 nm. Blue channel was collected at 420–550 nm and green channel was collected at 520–570 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM, XRD, XPS, IR and UV–vis of the CQDs-OH and CQDs-O-NBD
ce:section.ce:para: First, the prepared CQDs-OH was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. 1 a, the CQDs-OH particles are nearly spherical and monodisperse. The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (the inset of Fig. 1a) clearly shows that the lattice spacing of the carbon quantum dots is 0.21 nm, which is consistent with the in-plane lattice (100) of graphitic carbon [48,60]. The average diameter of the CQDs-OH (based on 100 particles) is 5.20 ± 0.93 nm in the range from 3.13 to 8.25 nm (Fig. 1b). The XRD diffraction pattern (Fig. 1c) of the prepared CQDs-OH shows a main diffraction peak (002) at ca. 2θ = 25°, which further confirms that the CQDs-OH has a graphite structure and a good crystallinity [48,61].

The surface composition and functional groups of the prepared CQDs-OH were studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2 a) shows that the CQDs-OH mainly contain two elements, C and O, having the contents of 76.96% and 23.04%, respectively. Two peaks correspond to 284.6 and 531.5 eV, respectively, which belong to C 1s and O 1s. In detail, the high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 2b) of C 1s shows three peaks at 287.1, 286.2 and 284.9 eV, respectively, corresponding to CO (carbonyl carbon), C–O (sp3 carbon) and C–C/CC (graphic carbon) groups [62].

The O 1s high resolution spectrum (Fig. 2c) splits into two peaks at bond energies of 531.7 and 533.2 eV, which were attributed to CO (carboxyl) and C–OH (hydroxy oxygen) groups [63,64], respectively. The percents of C–OH and CO were calculated to be 70.95% and 29.95%, respectively, which indicates that there is a large amount of –OH on the surface of CQDs-OH. It can explain the good solubility of the prepared CQDs-OH in water. The Fourier infrared spectrum of CQDs-OH in Fig. 2d showed that there was a wide and large absorption peak at 3425 cm−1, which is attributed to the stretching vibration peak of O–H on the surface of the CQDs-OH. Two sharp peaks at 1614 and 1434 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration peak of CC (benzene skeleton) [33]. The position of stretching vibration peak of C–O is assigned to about 1136 cm−1. FT-IR and XPS together confirmed that the CQDs-OH have graphic core structure and a large number of –OH on the surface.

The optical properties of the CQDs-OH in ethanol/PBS aqueous solution (vethanol/vPBS = 1/1, CPBS = 10 mM and pH = 7.4) were studied by using UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 3 a, there are two absorption peaks of the CQDs-OH at 225 nm and 370 nm, respectively, which can be attributed to π→π* of aromatic ring and n→π* of CO [62,65]. Furthermore, the emission peaks of the CQDs-OH under different excitation wavelength lights were studied as shown in Fig. 3b. When the excitation wavelength gradually increased from 317 nm to 397 nm, the position of the emission peak at 467 nm did not change. The QY of CQDs-OH can be calculated to be 19.3%. It can be seen from the 3D fluorescence spectrum (in Fig. 3c) of the CQDs-OH that its maximum excitation and emission wavelengths are 377 nm and 467 nm, respectively. The above results show that the emission wavelength of the CQDs-OH is independent of the excitation wavelengths, indicating that the fluorescence emission of the CQDs-OH is mainly caused by the surface emission mechanism [53]. It can be seen from the absorption and excitation spectra (in Fig. 3a) of the CQDs-OH that the maximum excitation wavelength (377 nm) of the CQDs-OH is mainly caused by its absorption at 370 nm. That is, the fluorescence center of the CQDs-OH is identical with its absorption structure. These results further confirm the consistency of optical properties of the prepared CQDs-OH [33].

The results of fluorescence lifetime are shown in Fig. 3d. The fluorescence lifetimes of CQDs-OH and CQDs-O-NBD are derived to be 2.05 ns (τ0) and 1.92 ns (τ) at 377 nm excitation wavelength, respectively. NBD group can quench the fluorescence of CQDs by dynamic and/or static quenching. The QY of CQDs-O-NBD can be calculated to be 3.7%. Theoretically, for static quenching, τ0/τ = 1; for dynamic quenching, F0/F = τ0/τ. In this paper, the τ0/τ > 1 and the F0/F ≠ τ0/τ, which indicates that both static quenching and dynamic quenching exist in this processes [41]. Because the τ is closed to τ0, the static quenching effect was probably dominant in this system. The difference of UV–vis absorption spectra between CQDs and CQDs-O-NBD in Fig. 3a also supports the existence of static quenching mechanism. There are two ways of dynamic quenching: electron transfer (PET) and energy transfer. Fig. 3a shows that the UV–vis absorption spectrum of NBD group does not overlap with the emission spectrum of CQDs-OH, which indicates that the fluorescence quenching is not caused by energy transfer. In conclusion, the fluorescence quenching mechanism is mainly attributed to both static quenching and PET effect in the present processes.

The surface composition and functional groups of the carbon quantum dot-O-7-nitrobenzo-2-oxa-1,3-diazole (CQDs-O-NBD) were confirmed by using FT-IR and XPS. It can be seen from the survey spectrum (in Fig. 4 a) that the CQDs-O-NBD contains a new element, N 1s (400.3 eV), and its content is 9.05%. The N 1s high resolution spectrum is split into four peaks (in Fig. 4b): pyridinic N (400.0 eV), N–C (400.1 eV), N–O (404.7 eV), –NO2 (405.4 eV) [33,41,53], indicating that NBD was successfully modified on the surface of the CQDs-OH. The peaks at 1539 and 1330 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum (in Fig. 2d) of the CQDs-O-NBD and NBD-Cl are attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of –NO2 [53], respectively. Furthermore, the wide and large characteristic stretching vibration peak (3425 cm−1) of –OH on the CQDs-OH surface almost disappeared, which indicates that CQDs-OH have reacted with NBD-Cl and its surface was successfully modified by NBD-groups. The above results clearly indicate that the CQDs-O-NBD were successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical responses of the CQDs-O-NBD to Cys/Hcy and GSH/H2S
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the optical responses of the CQDs-O-NBD to Cys/Hcy and GSH/H2S in ethanol/PBS aqueous solution (vethanol/vPBS = 1/1, CPBS = 10 mM and pH = 7.4) were investigated by using UV–vis spectra. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the CQDs-O-NBD and CQDs-OH present same absorption peaks at 370 nm, indicating that the absorption peak of the CQDs-O-NBD was mainly caused by the CQDs-OH. When biothiols were added, the formed CQDs-O-NBD + Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS solutions present the absorption peaks at 370 nm too, which were attributed to the release of CQDs-OH after the reactions between the CQDs-O-NBD and Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS. When GSH was added, no more peaks appeared. When Cys/Hcy and NaHS were added, the absorption peaks appeared at 467 nm and 540 nm, respectively. According to the literatures [44,66], the absorption peak at 467 nm belongs to the characteristic absorption peak of NBD-N-Cys/Hcy, and the absorption peak at 540 nm is attributed to the characteristic absorption peak of NBD-SH. Under the natural light, the formed CQDs-O-NBD + NaHS solution appears pink color as shown in Fig. 5b, while the formed CQDs-O-NBD + Cys/Hcy/GSH solutions appear bright yellow. So, the NaHS can be identified by naked eye test. Under the irradiation of the ultraviolet lamp, the formed CQDs-O-NBD + Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS solutions is yellow-green, as shown in Fig. 5b, which can prove that the CQDs-O-NBD and Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS have reacted. At the same time, the CQDs are released and the fluorescences are restored.

Subsequently, the fluorescence spectra of the CQDs-O-NBD after reactions with Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS in ethanol/PBS aqueous solution (vethanol/vPBS = 1/1, CPBS = 10 mM and pH = 7.4) were studied. As expected, when excited at 377 nm wavelength light, the fluorescence of the CQDs-O-NBD was very weak compared to the CQDs-OH (in Fig. 5c).

After adding Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS to the CQDs-O-NBD solution, the ether bonds were broken along with the release of the fluorescent carbon qutumn dots and generation of NBD-derivatives. When the excitation wavelength was 377 nm, as shown in Fig. 5c, the fluorescence intensity of the formed CQDs-O-NBD + Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS solution at 467 nm obviously increased. Fig. 5c also showed that the fluorescence of the probe is not completely recovered in the presence of biothiols and HS−. It can be seen from Figs. 3a and 5a that there is a less overlap area between the fluorescence emission spectrum of CQDs and the UV–vis absorption spectra of NBD-biothiols, which implied that there was almost no FRET effect between CQDS and NBD-biothiols/H2S.

When the excitation wavelength was 470 nm, as shown in Fig. 5d, only the formed solution of CQDs-O-NBD + Cys/Hcy showed an significantly emission peak at 546 nm. This was because a new fluorescent substance NBD-N-Cys or NBD-N-Hcy was generated. The above results clearly showed that the CQDs-O-NBD as a probe can selectively identify Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS at 467 nm; and can distinguish Cys/Hcy from GSH/NaHS at 546 nm.

In order to evaluate the ability of the CQDs-O-NBD as a probe to quantify Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS in ethanol/PBS aqueous solution (vethanol/vPBS = 1/1, CPBS = 10 mM and pH = 7.4), the fluorescence spectra of the CQDs-O-NBD solution were recorded after adding different concentrations of Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS, as shown in Fig. 6 a–d. Taking Cys as an example, when the excitation wavelength light is 377 nm, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs-O-NBD solution gradually increases as the concentration of Cys increases. When the concentration of Cys reaches 30 μM, the fluorescence intensity reaches equilibrium. The fluorescence intensity of the CQDs-O-NBD solution has a good linear relationship with Cys concentrations in the range of 0.5–15 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) calculated based on 3σ/k is 0.24 μM, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank measurement, and k is the linear slope of fluorescence intensity of the CQDs-O-NBD solution versus Cys concentrations. For Hcy/GSH/NaHS, their fluorescent responses to the CQDs-O-NBD are similar to Cys (in Fig. 6b–d). The linear concentration ranges were 0.5–10 μM, 0.5–12.5 μM and 0–10 μM, and the corresponding LODs were 0.21 μM, 0.11 μM and 0.18 μM for Hcy, GSH and NaHS, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 7 , when the excitation wavelength is 470 nm, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs-O-NBD solution have the good linear relationships with the concentrations of Cys and Hcy. The concentration ranges of Cys and Hcy are 0.5–15 μM and 0.5–25 μM, respectively. The corresponding LODs are 0.07 μM and 0.06 μM. According to the literatures [37,67], the levels of GSH, Cys and Hcy in normal cells are 1–15 mM, 30–200 μM, and 5–12 μM, respectively, and the H2S content in blood is 5–100 μM. The LODs of CQDs-O-NBD for Cys and Hcy were all lower than the corresponding concentrations of intracellular Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS. These results indicate that the CQDs-O-NBD as a probe has high sensitivity and selectivity to Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS at excitation wavelengths of 377 nm and 470 nm, and can be used for quantitative and qualitative detection of Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS.

As shown in Table S1, this probe was compared with previously reported fluorescent probe for the detection of biothiols. It can be seen that our method had relative low detection limit and similar linear range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time and pH to fluorescence responses of the CQDs-O-NBD
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the time dependence of CQDs-O-NBD on Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS, a series of studies were conducted. As shown in Fig. 8 , first, the fluorescence intensity of the probe CQDs-O-NBD did not change with the increase of time under the excitation of 377 nm and 470 nm wavelength light, respectively, indicating that the probe has good stability. After Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS was added to the CQDs-O-NBD solution, the fluorescence intensity of the resulting solution increased significantly and gradually reached equilibrium as the reaction time increased under the dual excitation wavelengths. The time required for the fluorescence intensity of the formed solution CQDs-O-NBD + Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS to reach equilibrium under 377 nm excitation wavelength light is 120 min, and the time required for the fluorescence intensity of the formed solution CQDs-O-NBD + Cys/Hcy to reach equilibrium under 470 nm excitation wavelength light is 60 min.

The effects of pH on the determination of Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS were studied, and the results were shown in Fig. 9 . In the range of pH = 4–10, it can be observed that the fluorescence intensities of the CQDs-O-NBD change little, which provides a basis for detection Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS under physiological condition. As the pH gradually increases, the nucleophilicity of biothiols gradually increases, and the fluorescence intensities of the CQDs-O-NBD solution were obviously enhanced at pH = 7–9 upon excitation at 377 nm and 470 nm wavelength lights, respectively. Therefore, the CQDs-O-NBD responds well to Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS in a wide range of pH (pH = 7–9) at the double excitations. Collectively, the CQDs-O-NBD as probe can be used to distinguish the Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS at the excitations of 377 nm and 470 nm wavelength lights under the physiological conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence selectivity of the CQDs-O-NBD as a probe in Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS detection
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is the most important characteristic of fluorescent probes. In order to study the selectivity of the CQDs-O-NBD to Cys/Hcy and GSH/NaHS, the fluorescence spectra of other amino acids and ions coexisting in organisms were studied under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 10 , when the excitation light is 377 nm, the fluorescence intensities of the CQDs-O-NBD solution do not change significantly after the coexisting substances are added. It shows that the most of the coexisting substances do not react with the CQDs-O-NBD and the ether bonds of the CQDs-O-NBD are not destroyed. However, ClO− can further quench the probe. Taking into account the oxidizing properties of ClO−, we also investigated the effects of several common ROS on the probe, as shown in Fig. S1. The results indicate that the common ROS have no quenching effect on the probe.

The fluorescence intensities of the CQDs-O-NBD increase significantly after adding the Cys/Hcy and GSH/NaHS, which indicate that there are reactions between Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS and the CQDs-O-NBD, and the ether bonds are broken and the carbon dots are released. When the excitation wavelength is 470 nm, only Cys and Hcy can cause significant fluorescent signal increases at 546 nm. Other analytes almost not change the fluorescence of the CQDs-O-NBD. Futhermore, the competition experiments are shown in Fig. S2, which indicated that most of the coexisting substances did not significantly interfere with the detection of biological biothiols and HS−. These results show that under complex physiological conditions, the CQDs-O-NBD as a probe has high selective for biothiols than other analytes when emitted at 467 nm wavelength light, and can distinguish Cys/Hcy from GSH/NaHS when emitted at 546 nm wavelength light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism of Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS by using CQDs-O-NBD
ce:section.ce:para: The NBD-derivatives of Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS have distinct optical properties [3,36,68]. In addition, the nucleophilic property of Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS is better than that of other amino acids. Thus, Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS can react with the fluorophore-O-NBD through nucleophilic aromatic substitution (SNAr) [46]. For these reasons, the CQDs-O-NBD was designed and synthesized.

The proposed reaction mechanism of CQDs-O-NBD with Cys/Hcy and GSH/NaHS is shown in Fig. 11 . Through the reaction of 4-chloro-7-nitrobenzo-2-oxa-1,3-diazole (NBD-Cl) with the phenolic hydroxyl groups of the carbon quantum dots (CQDs), NBD groups were introduced on the surface of the CQDs. That is, the new substance CQDs-O-NBD is formed via ether linkage between the CQDs and NBD groups. The fluorescence of the CQDs-O-NBD is very weak compared to CQDs-OH (as shown in Fig. 11). This is because there are strong electron withdrawing parts in NBD group. The fluorescence of the CQDs-O-NBD is mainly quenched through the static quenching (SQ) and photo-induced electron transfer (PET) between CQD and NBD [41,42]. After adding Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS, the ether bonds of CQDs-O-NBD are broken, releasing the blue fluorescent CQDs-OH and generating new nonfluorescent NBD-derivatives. The formation of four NBD derivatives was further confirmed by UPLC-MS. The mass spectra are shown in the supporting documents (Fig. S3-6). The spectrum peaks of NBD-Cys/Hcy/GSH/HS can be clearly seen from the mass spectrum (m/z = 283.0141 for NBD-N-Cys, [M − 1]−1; m/z = 297.0296 for NBD-N-Hcy, [M − 1]−1; m/z = 469.0775 for NBD-S-GSH, [M − 1]−1; and m/z = 195.9819 for NBD-SH, [M − 1]−1). Pluth [46] and Song [69] et al. confirmed that the product NBD-S-Cys/Hcy are nonflurescent compounds，but they can rapidly rearranged to form NBD-N-Cys/Hcy, which emits a green fluorescence at 546 nm. Due to the steric hindrance of GSH and the far distance between N and S, there is no intramolecular rearrangement of NBD-S-GSH (no green fluorescence at 546 nm), and that the formed NBD-SH is a nonflurescent compound. In short, when Cys/Hcy was added, two fluorescence signals were detected at 467 nm (channel 1) and 546 nm (channel 2), respectively. However, after the addition of GSH/NaHS, only 467 nm (channel 1) of a fluorescence signal was detected. Therefore, the CQDs-O-NBD can be used as a double excitation and double emission fluorescent probe for the detection of Cys/Hcy/GSH/H2S at 467 nm and the identification of Cys/Hcy and GSH/H2S at 546 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CQDs-O-NBD in biological system
ce:section.ce:para: In view of the excellent spectral responses of the CQDs-O-NBD to Cys/Hcy and GSH/NaHS, the feasibility of the CQDs-O-NBD for the detection of Cys/Hcy/GSH/NaHS in fetal bovine serum (FBS) was investigated. Although Hcy and Cys have similar fluorescence responses to the CQDs-O-NBD, the concentration (5–12 μM) of Hcy in serum is far lower than that (30–200 μM) of Cys. Therefore, the interference of endogenous Hcy can be ignored in the analysis of Cys in actual serum samples.

According to reference [70], the disulfides in FBS were converted into free mercaptan compounds by using triphenylphosphine. And then, the converted FBS was diluted 50 times to ensure that Cys concentration meet the requirements of linear detection. By the standard addition method [71,72], the different concentrations of Cys (2, 6 and 10 μM) were added to the CQDs-O-NBD and FBS solution and the corresponding fluorescence intensities were determined at 546 nm. Based on the calibration equation mentioned above, each added concentration was calculated and the satisfactory recoveries (in the range of 99.7–103.2%) were obtained. In order to verify the detection accuracy of the designed CQDs-O-NBD, Ellman method was used for comparison as shown in Table 1 . It can be seen from Table 1 that the measured results are consistent with these obtained by Ellman method, indicating that the CQDs-O-NBD as a probe has the ability to quantitatively detect the Cys in real serum samples, and the measured results are accurate and reliable.

Since Cys/Hcy and GSH/NaHS have similar fluorescence responses to CQDs-O-NBD probes respectively, only the imaging ability of probes for Cys and GSH in living cells was studied in the fluorescence confocal imaging experiment.

Firstly, MTT assay was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the CQDs-O-NBD to HeLa cells. As shown in Fig. 12 , when the concentration of the CQDs-O-NBD increased from 0 to 50 μg mL−1, the cell viability decreased slightly. Even when the concentration of the CQDs-O-NBD reached 50 μg mL−1, the cell viability was still more than 80%, illustrating that Hela cells still can maintain a good state under the high concentration of the CQDs-O-NBD. The above results show that the CQDs-O-NBD as a probe has low cytotoxicity to HeLa cells and good biocompatibility. It can be predicted that the CQDs-O-NBD as a probe is promising for cell imaging experiments.

Firstly, HeLa cells were incubated in the CQDs-O-NBD solution (20 μg mL−1) for 4.0 h. As shown in Fig. 13 a–d, the fluorescences of HeLa cells could be observed in blue and green channels, which indicated that the CQDs-O-NBD could penetrate into HeLa cell and react with endogenous thiols to produce obvious fluorescence responses. In order to eliminate the influence of biothiols in the cells, HeLa cells were treated with NEM (1 mM) (a well-known thiol blocker) for 40 min, and then incubated together with the CQDs-O-NBD (20 μg mL−1) for another 4.0 h. In Fig. 13e–h, the fluorescence is hardly observed in both blue and green channels. In order to further evaluate the fluorescence response of the CQDs-O-NBD as a probe to exogenous Cys and GSH, the following experiments were carried out. HeLa cells were pretreated with NEM (1 mM for 40 min), cultured with the CQDs-O-NBD (20 μg mL−1) for another 4.0 h, and then incubated with Cys (100 μM) or GSH (100 μM) for 1.0 h. As shown in Fig. 13i-l, HeLa cells with Cys showed obvious fluorescence responses in both blue and green channels. HeLa cells with GSH only showed an obvious fluorescence in blue channel (Fig. 13m-p), while no fluorescence in green channel was observed.

The above results clearly show that the green fluorescences result from NBD-N-Cys generated through the selective triggering of Cys towards CQDs-O-NBD, while the blue fluorescences are caused by the CQDs-OH released in the CQDs-O-NBD and Cys/GSH reaction. Therefore, the CQDs-O-NBD as a probe has a good sensing performance and can distinguish Cys and GSH in living cells by double fluorescence signals.

In summary, based on carbon quantum dots a novel dual excitation and dual emission fluorescent probe, CQDs-O-NBD, has been synthesized. The probe has high sensitivity and selectivity for analysis of biothiols and H2S. Channel 1 (λex/em = 377/467 nm) can be used to identify Cys/Hcy/GSH/H2S from other substances. Channel 2 (λex/em = 470/546 nm) can be used to discriminate Cys/Hcy from GSH/H2S. The CQDs-O-NBD was successfully applied to detect the Cys content in fetal bovine serum. Meanwhile, the CQDs-O-NBD has low cytotoxicity and can be used for imaging in Hela cells, indicating that it has a good potential in biomedical applications.
